entries.author: St. Dionysius of Alexandria
entries.title: EXCESSIVE COMPARISON.
entries.body: When a man has bought a large enough field and sees that his neighbor’s is larger still, he wants to increase his own so as to make his house greater. {fn:0}

entries.author: Andreas
entries.title: THE TEST OF TEACHING.
entries.body: James shows that the teaching{fn:0} is not working, for they are all carnal and doing the most wicked things. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: The skewed teaching that the people have received.

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: THE STRUGGLE WITHIN.
entries.body: Your passions are at war in your members whenever your hands or your tongue or some combination of your bodily parts obeys the promptings of your depraved mind. It is also possible that the passions mentioned here are in fact good desires, pointing towards the riches and benefits of God’s kingdom. On account of these and many other such things there is often a struggle between good and evil going on in our minds. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: ASK FOR RIGHT MOTIVES.
entries.body: The reason that you struggle but fail to obtain what you want is that you do not ask God to give these things to you. For if you ask him with the right motives, he will give you not only everything you need here on earth but also what you need to get to heaven. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: THE OPPOSITE OBTAINED FROM WRONG DESIRES.
entries.body: James proceeds here by way of thesis and antithesis. The thesis, that is, what they desire, is absurd to begin with, but the way they go about getting what they want ensures the exact opposite. Murder and fighting are not good things, but neither do the good things which they desire follow from them. Note also that here James speaks of murder and of fighting as spiritual things, not physical ones. It would be bad enough to think this kind of thing about robbers, but how much worse it is when we are dealing with people who have a certain amount of faith and who have turned to God. These are people who are trying to kill the soul and to fight against godliness. {fn:0}

entries.author: Didymus the Blind
entries.title: ASK IN THE RIGHT SPIRIT.
entries.body: The Savior said: Ask and you will receive. Everyone who asks will receive.{fn:0} How can it be then that some people pray but do not get what they ask for? To this it must be answered that if someone comes to prayer in the right way, omitting none of the prerequisites for intercession, he will receive everything he asks for. But if someone appears to be going beyond the permissible bounds laid down for intercession, he will appear to be asking for something in the wrong way and therefore will not obtain it. {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: ABUSIVE INTENTIONS.
entries.body: If someone intends to misuse what he receives, he will not receive it. Instead, God will pity him. {fn:0}

entries.author: Andreas
entries.title: GOD GRASPS OUR WORTHINESS TO RECEIVE.
entries.body: It appears that some ask but do not receive. God ignores those who attack him and those who ask wrongly, according to their own desires. But someone will say that even those who ask for divine wisdom and virtue do not receive them. In reply it must be said that such people may be worthy to receive these good things, but they must do so in the right way. Perhaps they want such things merely for the pleasure of having them, and if so, they will not get them. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite
entries.title: ASKING FOR WHAT PLEASES GOD.
entries.body: The hierarch,{fn:0} being a man of God, asks only for what is compatible with the divine promises, for what pleases God and for what God will freely give. He thereby demonstrates to God the lover of good that his own conduct is always modeled on the Good and shows those who are present what kinds of gifts the saints will receive. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: One who stands within the right ordering of God’s providential design.

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: FALSE PETITIONS.
entries.body: Those who continue in their sins ask wrongly. They entreat the Lord ill-advisedly to forgive them sins which they are not prepared to forgive in others. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: VIRTUE AND EVIL.
entries.body: Since evil forms a friendship with the world and virtue a friendship with God, virtue and evil cannot coexist. {fn:0}

entries.author: Didymus the Blind
entries.title: GOD AND MAMMON OPPOSITES.
entries.body: Whoever loves the world by committing sin is revealed as an enemy of God, just as, on the other hand, one who affirms friendship with God by not sinning is a constant enemy of the world. Therefore, just as it is impossible to serve both God and mammon,{fn:0} so it is also impossible to be a friend of the world and of God at the same time. {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: EVEN THE SON FACED THESE ENMITIES.
entries.body: It was because of these enmities toward God that not even his only-begotten Son was spared. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Hilary of Arles
entries.title: THE UNDOING OF THE FALLEN ANGELS.
entries.body: Love of honor and pride and boastfulness is hostile to God, for these things were the undoing of the fallen angels as well as of the first human couple, which is why to this day they are described as enemies of God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Introductory Tractate on the Letter of James

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: TOLERATING ADULTEROUS TEACHERS.
entries.body: James calls these people adulterers, not because they practiced physical adultery but because they corrupted the commands which were instituted by God and turned away to other loves. They were even prepared to tolerate an adulterous teacher, even if it was clear that he was as deep in the mud as any pig. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Symeon the New Theologian
entries.title: ENMITY TENDS TOWARD BLINDNESS.
entries.body: When one is at enmity toward someone else, he has no idea how to explain to others what that person approves of or likes, nor is he able to instruct them concerning his desires. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: THE SPIRIT DRAWS US TOWARD GOD.
entries.body: What this means is that the Spirit in us tends toward fellowship with God. He turns us away from the love of the world and gives us ever more grace. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: WHETHER THIS IS THE HUMAN SPIRIT.
entries.body: Some think that this refers to God’s Spirit, which does battle inside us against envy and tries to rid us of it. Others, however, think that it refers to the human spirit and is a warning to us not to desire or to attach ourselves to the lusts of this world, because as long as the spirit of our mind desires earthly things it does so with envy, because it envies the fact that others have what it wants to obtain. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: USE WISDOM RIGHTLY.
entries.body: James is here using an abbreviated form of speech in order to convey his meaning. What he is saying is this: I have thus far been warning you in my own words to make the right and proper use of your wisdom that you might not abuse it in your pride by corrupting it or misinterpreting it by your overly clever preaching. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Clement of Rome
entries.title: DRAW NEAR TO GRACIOUS PEOPLE.
entries.body: Scripture says that God resists the arrogant but gives grace to the humble. We should associate with those to whom God’s grace has been given. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: AGAINST THOSE WHO DEMEAN MARRIAGE.
entries.body: There are those who say openly that marriage is fornication. They lay it down as a dogma that it was instituted by the devil. They are arrogant and claim that they are emulating the Lord, who did not marry and had no worldly possessions. It is their boast that they have a deeper understanding of the gospel than anyone else. To them Scripture says that God is against the proud and gives grace to the humble. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WARNING AGAINST SELF-CONCEIT.
entries.body: Give me someone professing perpetual continence, who is free from all vices and blemishes of conduct. For her I fear pride—I dread the swelling of self-conceit from so great a blessing. The more there is in her which she is satisfied with, the more I fear that in pleasing herself she will displease the one who resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Jerome
entries.title: ARROGANCE.
entries.body: One who holds his head high in arrogance hates God. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Macarius of Egypt
entries.title: THE TRUE SIGN OF A CHRISTIAN.
entries.body: The true sign of a Christian is the following: to feed the hungry and give drink to the thirsty, to endure hunger and thirst, to be poor in spirit, humble and contemptible in one’s own eyes. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
entries.title: GOD WANTS TO DWELL IN YOU.
entries.body: Be humble, in order that God may rest in you, which he wants to do. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Valerian of Cimiez
entries.title: PRIDE AND HUMILITY.
entries.body: One man is invited to grace in proportion to his love of humility. Another is consigned to punishment in proportion to his sin of pride. So if the swelling of pride is taking place in anyone, let him combat it, lest he draw the arms of heavenly justice against himself. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: THE PROUD TRUST THEIR OWN STRENGTH.
entries.body: God punishes robbers, perjurers, gluttons and other sinners because they are in contempt of his commandments, but it is said that he resists the proud in a special way. This is because those who trust in their own strength, who neglect to submit themselves to God’s power, who really think that they can almost save themselves and there-fore have no time to seek help from above—these are all deserving of greater punishment. On the other hand, God gives grace to the humble because they recognize their need and ask him for help to overcome the plague of their sins, and for this reason they deserve to be healed. It ought to be noted that James quotes this verse from Proverbs according to the Septuagint, as does Peter in his letter. The Latin text, which is based on the Hebrew original, reads: Toward the scorners he is scornful, but to the humble he shows favor.{fn:0} {fn:1}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: ENMITY WITH GOD.
entries.body: It is not absurd to say that contempt for divine doctrine and an inordinate love for the world derive from pride and are the substance of enmity against God. God resists the proud, because it is normal to resist one’s enemies, and the proud must be counted among them. {fn:0}

entries.author: Andreas
entries.title: GIVING MORE GRACE.
entries.body: If death came into the world by the malice of the devil, and Christ dwells in the inner man according to the Scriptures, this is the reason why he dwells in us, that he might destroy the death which has come upon us through the devil’s cunning. And not only this, but that he might give us more grace as well. For he said: I have come that they might have life, and have it more abundantly.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
entries.title: FIGHT AGAINST CAPTIVITY.
entries.body: Let us fight as hard as we can, with the Lord’s help, against that most harsh captivity of the soul [which is the devil’s ability to divert our thoughts away from spiritual concerns]. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: GOD IS NEAR.
entries.body: God is near, and he does not drive away those who draw near to him. {fn:0}
entries.citation: The Prayer of Job and David 3.11.29

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: NEARNESS TO GOD.
entries.body: Draw near to God in humility, by walking in his footsteps, and he will draw near to you in his mercy, setting you free from all anxiety. For nobody is far away from God in terms of physical distance; the problem is one of attitudes and emotions. For the person who is anxious to do what is right is always near to God, whereas the one who is lost in his wickedness is far away from him, regardless of where either one happens to live. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: DOUBLE-MINDED.
entries.body: James describes as double-minded those who do not want to live in a committed way but who are tossed about by the iniquities of men. It is clear from what Job says that the mind here stands for our very life: skin for skin, [Satan says], all that a man has he will give for his own soul.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: FIRST, CONFESS.
entries.body: The person who repents after sinning is worthy of blessings, not of mourning, as he returns to the company of the righteous. First, confess your sins that you may be justified, for if someone is not ashamed of his sin he is miserable, not so much because he fell from grace but because he has remained in his fallen state. And if it is a wicked thing not to repent after sinning, what punishment will someone deserve who sins as a matter of course? If a person overcome with the need to repent is unclean, what forgiveness will there be for someone who suffers because he remains in his sins? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Leander of Seville
entries.title: MOURN YOUR SINS.
entries.body: Flee laughter as a sin{fn:0} and change temporal joy into mourning so that you may be blessed, for those who mourn are blessed and shall be comforted.{fn:1} {fn:2}

footnotes.text: When repenting, your mind should be at the greatest distance from an attitude that laughs about the sin.

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: READINESS TO REPENT.
entries.body: Do not rejoice in the things of this world, but remember the sins which you have committed and spend the short time which is allotted to you on this earth looking for the joys of the heavenly kingdom. You do not want to find that your pursuit of earthly enjoyment produces a situation in which you will be a beggar in eternity, weeping and wailing forever in your torment. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: Didymus the Blind
entries.title: COMBATING PRIDE.
entries.body: Pride is the greatest of all evils. To the extent that humility can oppose it, it is a great good. And when both of these are consciously and deliberately at work, good I mean and evil, everyone who humbles himself before God and rejects the proud will be raised up, and his humility will take him to the heights.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Hesychius of Jerusalem
entries.title: THE HUMBLE ARE BLESSED.
entries.body: It is a blessed thing to humble oneself before the Lord. For James says: Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will exalt you. Whenever we are thus humbled, even if we are tempted by demons and even if we are attacked by those who hate virtue, we have God to deliver us, as long as we do not forget his law or curse him in our sufferings. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Alexandria
entries.title: WICKED ACTS GIVE BIRTH TO ARROGANCE.
entries.body: Every wicked act dulls the sense of our thoughts and gives birth to arrogance. For although it is necessary for each one to examine himself and behave according to God’s will, many people do not do this but prefer to mind the business of others. If they happen to see others suffering, it seems that they forget their own weaknesses and set about criticizing them and slandering them. They condemn them, not knowing that they suffer from the same things as the people they have criticized, and in so doing they condemn themselves. The wise Paul writes exactly the same thing: If you judge another in something, you condemn yourself, for the one who judges does the same things.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: PRIDE ARISES FROM CONTEMPT.
entries.body: James knows that haughtiness and pride arise from contempt and disdain toward the meek, which pushes those who behave that way to despise them completely. He wants to turn his hearers aside from this. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Hilary of Arles
entries.title: ONLY ONE LAWGIVER.
entries.body: The law of the Bible was given through many agents, like Moses and Elijah and John the Baptist, but ultimately it is still only one law, and there is only one lawgiver. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Introductory Tractate on the Letter of James

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: THE ARROGANCE OF JUDGING.
entries.body: James says it is arrogant to judge another person and not to consider the uncertain state of our own weakness and time-bound life. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Concerning the Epistle of St. James

entries.author: Theophylact of Ohrid
entries.title: DO NOT DESPISE THE LAW.
entries.body: Who would endure having to live under a law which he despises? Therefore do not be a despiser of the law, says James, and do not look for some other legislator, who will prescribe the opposite. For there is only one lawgiver, God, who can both condemn and deliver sinners. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Alexandria
entries.title: PUT YOUR LIFE IN PERSPECTIVE.
entries.body: Some people go on endless journeys for the sake of business and the profits which they can make thereby, enduring even sea travel for their sake. Some fight in order to get some advantage over others by increasing their power. Still others fatten their purses by cheating and by extortion, bringing down fire and brimstone on their heads. {fn:0}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: THE WIDER PLAN.
entries.body: James does not take away our free will but points out that everything we do is part of a wider plan which is governed by God’s grace. Even if we are able to run around and get on with the business of this life, we must not attribute this ability to our own efforts but accept that we can do these things only by the blessing of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE VAPOR OF TEMPORAL GOODS.
entries.body: Restoring health for a time to a man’s body amounts to no more than extending his breath for a little while longer. Therefore it should not be considered of great importance, because it is temporal, not eternal. {fn:0}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: OUR TEMPORAL LABOR VANISHES.
entries.body: James says this in order to indicate just how fleeting and empty our present life is. He wants to make us ashamed of the fact that we spend all our time engaged in its vanity, and in the evils of this age and in things which, as soon as they are accomplished, disappear, and all our labor vanishes with them. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: GRACE COMPLEMENTS HUMAN EFFORT.
entries.body: James is not trying to take away our freedom to decide, but he is showing us that it is not just what we want that matters. We need God’s grace to complement our efforts and ought to rely not on them but on God’s love for us. As it says in Proverbs: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring forth.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: IN BAPTISM WE RENOUNCE THE DEVIL.
entries.body: Vain boasting comes from pride, and its ultimate source is the devil. Those who have been baptized into Christ ought not to take any kind of inspiration from Satan. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: GRACE ENABLES DOING GOOD.
entries.body: James does not remove the power to do good, but he shows that it is not just a matter of one’s own will. To do good as we ought, we need the grace of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: KNOWING COMPLICATES SINNING.
entries.body: Does the one who does not know how to do good and does not do it commit a sin? He certainly does, but the one who knows what is good and does not do it sins more grievously. {fn:0}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: THE FAITH PROCLAIMED MUST BE LIVED.
entries.body: Good deeds ought to come before preaching, so that it will be clear that it is a righteous man who is proclaiming the faith which is being expounded. {fn:0}
